machine: per-measurement 'machine' field where present; entries without one were measured on one arm64 macOS machine (Darwin 24.5.0). Wall-clock timings are indicative context, not pinned quantities
caveat_emitted_vs_minimal: Every size below is the size of the proof EMITTED by the pinned pipeline — an achievable upper bound on proof length under that pipeline, NOT a minimal certificate. Minimal proof size is a proof-complexity quantity solvers do not compute; no claim about shortest proofs is made anywhere in this file.
family: R(3,k) upper half: the r3k-edge-cnf encoding (two pinned clause orders of the SAME clause set — see pipeline manifest) of 'K_n (n = R(3,k)) admits a 2-coloring with no red triangle and no blue K_k', which is UNSAT at n = R(3,k). Instances: (k=3, n=6), (k=4, n=9), (k=5, n=14 — lex pipeline; completed off-Mac at a 6 GiB cap after the 300 MB-cap DNFs proved to be cap artifacts).
growth_fit_note: No growth-law fit is claimed. Three family points are now completed (the charter's bare minimum): 247 B / ~564 KB pooled mean / 5.25-6.03 GB (2 seeds). Ratios: ~x2,284 then ~x10,000. A fit is still withheld: n=3 points, the R(3,5) order-variance axis is unmeasured, and cross-machine emitted-size comparability (arm64-darwin vs aarch64-linux) is untested at small instances. Relative seed spread grows with size: 0% (R33, degenerate) -> 2.4-5.4% (R34) -> ~13.8% over 2 seeds (R35). Points from different pipelines/machines are separate series, never mixed.

measurements.archived: certificates/ramsey-3-4/ (this seed's CNF + proof + negative control)

measurements.note: aborted when the emitted proof stream crossed the 300 MB cap (observed size at kill, 1 s polling); the completed emitted size is unknown but > 300 MB. Nothing was verified; no proof is archived.

the interleaved CNF is byte-identical to certificates/ramsey-3-3/problem.cnf (that file's sha256), and the emitted proof is byte-identical to the lex-pipeline proof and to the committed certificates/ramsey-3-3/proof.drat

measurements.machine: aarch64-linux (source-built CaDiCaL 3.0.0 at tag rel-3.0.0; drat-trim at the pinned commit; solver niced, verification memory-guarded)
measurements.note: drat-trim printed the s VERIFIED substring under an 18 GiB address-space guard. Proof retained off-repo (5.0 GiB); identity pinned by sha256 + deterministic regeneration from the pinned pipeline.

measurements.machine: aarch64-linux (source-built CaDiCaL 3.0.0 at tag rel-3.0.0; drat-trim at the pinned commit; solver niced, verification memory-guarded)
measurements.note: as seed 1; emitted size came within 7% of the 6 GiB cap.

measurements.machine: aarch64-linux (source-built CaDiCaL 3.0.0 at tag rel-3.0.0; drat-trim at the pinned commit; solver niced, verification memory-guarded)
measurements.note: aborted mid-solve by the operator after the run's disk-write/verification pressure twice destabilized the shared host (load events; production services co-resident). No seed-3 datum. Lesson recorded: gigabyte-scale solve+verify is dedicated-window work, not co-resident work.

r3_3_n6.note: the three proofs are byte-identical (same sha256) — at this scale the run is decided in preprocessing and never consults the seed, so the seed axis is degenerate here. The emitted proof is also byte-identical to the committed certificates/ramsey-3-3/proof.drat even though the generated CNF differs from the committed problem.cnf in clause order and comments — a single-instance stability observation, not a general claim.

r3_4_n9.note: seeds genuinely perturb the search at this scale; all three proofs verified

r3_5_n14.result: DNF at all three seeds (proof-size cap)

r3_3_n6.note: degenerate exactly as under lex — and the proof is byte-identical to the lex-pipeline proof: one sha256 across all six R(3,3) runs (2 orders x 3 seeds)

r3_5_n14.result: not attempted under this pipeline (Mac-tier tiny instances only; the lex pipeline's three k=5 attempts were all DNF at the 300 MB cap)

cross_encoding_comparison.question: Does clause ORDER move emitted size more or less than seed does? (Charter WS5: a family trend is meaningful only if it dominates the cross-config spread; this is the second variance axis the charter requires.)
cross_encoding_comparison.axes: order axis = lex vs interleaved (the SAME clause set under two pinned emission orders); seed axis = cadical --seed in {1,2,3} within one order

r3_3_n6.finding: both axes are degenerate: all six runs (2 orders x 3 seeds) emit the byte-identical 247-byte proof — one proof sha256 — even though the two CNF files differ in clause order and comments. At this scale the instance is decided before either the seed or the clause order can matter; the point measures pipeline stability, not variance.

r3_4_n9.per_seed_delta_caveat: seed pairing across encodings is not meaningful — the seed only indexes the solver's randomized decisions within one run, and there is no correspondence between 'seed 1 under lex' and 'seed 1 under interleaved'. The per-seed deltas are samples of the combined variation, not paired differences.
r3_4_n9.ranges_overlap: fully — the lex range [561523, 574869] lies entirely inside the interleaved range [547216, 577593]
r3_4_n9.finding: at this instance, clause order does NOT move emitted size more than seed does: the shift of per-order means (6,005 B, ~1.1% of the pooled mean 564,285 B) is smaller than the seed spread within either order (13,346 B / 30,377 B), and the two per-order seed ranges fully overlap. Both axes land in the same few-percent band. Three seeds per order — a coarse reading, stated without any significance claim.

r3_5_n14.note: no interleaved attempt (Mac-tier tiny instances only); the order axis at k=5 is unmeasured. The lex-pipeline DNF at the 300 MB cap stands on the seed axis only.

cross_encoding_comparison.implication_for_family_trends: the only cross-encoding spread measured so far is a few percent at R(3,4) and exactly zero at R(3,3) — but this axis has two points on one instance pair, and with only 2 completed family points no trend is read and no fit is claimed either way.

dnf_interpretation: The R(3,5) DNF is pipeline-relative, not a claim about the instance: under THIS encoding (no symmetry breaking), THIS solver/config, and THESE caps, the emitted plain-text DRAT exceeds 300 MB at every tried seed. A pipeline with symmetry breaking or a different encoding may well complete — that would be a measurement under a NEW pipeline_id, not a continuation of this curve.
